reasoning: The base rate of Iran officially closing or successfully interdicting the Strait of Hormuz to stop international shipping in a 1.5-month span is extremely low. Historically, Iran has made threats but backed off because closing the Strait hurts their own economy (blocking their oil exports) and would trigger overwhelming military backlash from the US and its allies. In June 2025, there was a massive spike in risk when the US joined Israel in bombing Iranian nuclear sites, causing Iran's parliament to back a measure to close the Strait, and prompting the loading of naval mines. But US military pressure remained intense, and by October 2025, no official closure or blockade had occurred. The Manifold Markets forecast was down to 5% by Oct 30, showing that despite peak tensions over the summer, the market judges the immediate threat to have subsided. With only ~50 days remaining until the December 31, 2025 deadline, and no recent news of actual transit disruptions or mine deployments, the chance of closure before year-end is very small. Following the crowd estimate and base rate, I predict 4%.
